title: Τέττιξ καὶ μύρμηκες
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 336 (= Perry 373). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Χειμῶνος ὥρᾳ τὸν σῖτον βραχέντα οἱ μύρμηκες ἔψυχον.
sentences.tr: Cheimōnos hōrāi ton siton brachenta hoi myrmēkes epsychon.
sentences.l: In-winter's season the grain having-been-wetted the ants were-drying.
sentences.i: One winter's day the ants were drying out their rain-soaked grain.
sentences.n: Χειμῶνος ὥρᾳ — a dative of time: 'in the season of winter.' βραχέντα is from βρέχω 'to wet' (the grain got rained on in storage). The 1927 printing drops the period after ἔψυχον; it is restored here.

sentences.t: Τέττιξ δὲ λιμώττων ᾔτει αὐτοὺς τροφήν.
sentences.tr: Tettix de limōttōn ēitei autous trophēn.
sentences.l: And a cicada, famishing, kept-asking them for-food.
sentences.i: A starving cicada came begging them for food.
sentences.n: λιμώττων — the same 'famished' participle that opened the Fox and the Grapes, masculine this time. αἰτέω takes two accusatives: ask someone for something. The τέττιξ is a cicada — the 'grasshopper' belongs to the English retellings.

sentences.t: Οἱ δὲ μύρμηκες εἶπον αὐτῷ· « Διὰ τί τὸ θέρος οὐ συνῆγες καὶ σὺ τροφήν; »
sentences.tr: Hoi de myrmēkes eipon autōi: « Dia ti to theros ou synēges kai sy trophēn; »
sentences.l: And the ants said to-him: « Because-of what through-the summer not were-you-gathering, you too, food? »
sentences.i: The ants said to him: « Why didn't you too gather food during the summer? »
sentences.n: διὰ τί, 'on account of what?' = why. τὸ θέρος is an accusative of duration: 'all summer long.' The Greek question mark is the semicolon (;).

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ εἶπεν· « Οὐκ ἐσχόλαζον, ἀλλ' ᾖδον μουσικῶς. »
sentences.tr: Ho de eipen: « Ouk escholazon, all' ēidon mousikōs. »
sentences.l: And he said: « Not was-I-at-leisure, but I-was-singing musically. »
sentences.i: « I had no leisure for that, » said he. « I was busy making music. »
sentences.n: ἐσχόλαζον — from σχολή, 'leisure' (the ancestor of 'school'): the cicada claims he had none to spare. ᾖδον is the imperfect of ᾄδω, 'sing' — song as a full-time occupation.

sentences.t: Οἱ δὲ γελάσαντες εἶπον· « Ἀλλ' εἰ θέρους ὥραις ηὔλεις, χειμῶνος ὀρχοῦ. »
sentences.tr: Hoi de gelasantes eipon: « All' ei therous hōrais ēuleis, cheimōnos orchou. »
sentences.l: And they, having-laughed, said: « Well, if in-summer's seasons you-were-piping, in-winter dance. »
sentences.i: They laughed and said: « Well then — if you piped away the summer, you can dance away the winter. »
sentences.n: θέρους … χειμῶνος — genitives of time: 'in summer… in winter.' The punchline is two words, ending on the imperative ὀρχοῦ, 'dance!' — αὐλέω (pipe) and ὀρχέομαι (dance) were the paired party skills; the ants make the music-lover finish the program.

sentences.t: Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι οὐ δεῖ τινα ἀμελεῖν ἐν παντὶ πράγματι, ἵνα μὴ λυπηθῇ καὶ κινδυνεύσῃ.
sentences.tr: Ho mythos dēloi hoti ou dei tina amelein en panti pragmati, hina mē lypēthēi kai kindyneusēi.
sentences.l: The fable shows that not must one be-careless in every matter, so-that not he-be-grieved and run-danger.
sentences.i: The fable shows that nothing should be treated carelessly, if grief and danger are to be avoided.
sentences.n: ἵνα μή + subjunctive — negative purpose: 'lest.' And note the verb of the moral: ἀμελεῖν, 'to neglect' — the same neglect that cost the hare his race and the wind his contest. The fables keep one moral vocabulary.
